title: Farmhouse Play Table
summary: A kid-height table from four store-bought legs, a premade panel top, and four aprons. The fastest real table you'll build.
description:
A children's play table that skips almost all the hard parts by buying two of them: turned end-table legs and a premade edge-glued project panel for the top. What's left is four 1x3 aprons that join the legs into a base — a Saturday-morning build with a pocket-hole jig.

The one thing to get right is pocket-hole orientation. Drill the pocket holes that will attach the tabletop so they face upward and sit on the inside faces of the aprons, hidden. Attach the short aprons to two legs each, join those two leg sets with the long aprons, then center the base under the premade panel and screw up into it from below. Because the top is premade and flat, the table comes out true as long as the base is square — check that before the final joint.

Store-bought legs make this look more finished than the effort suggests, which is exactly the point for a kid's table that has to survive markers, snacks, and standing on.

tools.note: Joins the aprons to the legs and the top to the base.
Only if you need to trim the aprons; a miter saw or hand saw works too.

materials.name: End-table legs
materials.note: Store-bought turned legs. Any 20–24" table leg suits a kid's height.

steps.title: Cut the aprons and drill the pocket holes
steps.body: Cut two short aprons 16 1/2" long and two long aprons 40 1/2" long from the 1x3. A 1x3's actual size is 3/4" x 2 1/2", so all four pieces share that cross-section and only the lengths differ. Square every crosscut, because the base is a plain rectangle and an out-of-square end shows up later as a table that rocks. Drill two pocket holes in each end of all four aprons for the leg joints, then drill a row of pocket holes along one long edge of each apron, all facing the same way, since those edge holes are the ones that will point upward on the inside faces to catch the tabletop. Check your lumber before you start: the four aprons need 114" of 1x3 and one 8 ft board is only 96", so buy two.

steps.title: Attach the short aprons
steps.body: Stand two legs on the bench and join them with one 16 1/2" short apron to form an end assembly, then repeat with the other two legs and the second short apron. Glue each joint and drive 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws through the apron ends into the legs, keeping the top edge of the apron flush with the top of the leg so the premade panel will later sit flat on all four. Before you drive anything, confirm the apron's row of edge pocket holes faces upward and lands on the inside face of the assembly, where it stays hidden and can still reach the tabletop. Getting this orientation right now saves a fight later.

steps.title: Join with the long aprons
steps.body: Set the two end assemblies on their sides and connect them with the two 40 1/2" long aprons, again glued and screwed with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws through the apron ends into the legs, top edges flush. Keep every long apron's upward-facing pocket holes on the inside of the base. Before the last joint is fully tightened, measure the base corner to corner both ways and adjust until the two diagonals match: the premade top is already flat, so any wobble in the finished table comes from a racked base.

steps.title: Attach the top
steps.body: Lay the 24" x 48" premade panel good-face-down on a padded surface, set the base upside down on it, and centre it with the tape so the overhang is equal end to end and front to back. Check your panel's thickness against the 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws before driving anything, then screw up through the aprons' upward-facing pocket holes into the underside of the top. Sand every edge and corner properly round rather than merely eased, because this is a kids' table and hands and chins meet those edges. Finish with a durable, wipeable coat that will survive markers, snacks, and being stood on.

images.alt: Kid-height farmhouse play table with turned legs and a plank-look top
